title: Dawsen Canopy or Poster Bed - Queen
summary: A queen bed built around purchased pencil-post legs, wrapped in 1x boards with a subtly angled headboard and footboard.
description:
This bed starts from a component you buy rather than build: turned pencil-post legs, sourced from a specialty wood-parts vendor, since square stock can't be turned into a proper post leg without a lathe. Everything else — wrapping the legs in 1x boards, building the headboard and footboard, assembling the rails — is standard pocket-hole joinery.

The headboard and footboard aren't built dead square: the boards are cut at a 1/2° off-square angle, which is barely perceptible by eye but is exactly why the plan insists on marking long-point and short-point on every one of those cuts. At half a degree, a board can look correct upside down or backwards and you won't catch it by sight — you'll only catch it by checking your marks. Finish the headboard, footboard, and rails separately, before final assembly, so the whole bed can still be carried into the bedroom in pieces rather than as one unliftable assembled frame.
timeLabel: 10–15 hrs, not counting delivery time for the purchased legs

tools.note: Precision on the 1/2° off-square cuts — mark long-point/short-point on every piece.

materials.name: Pre-turned bed post legs, ~80" length, 3-1/2" base
materials.note: Purchased from a specialty wood-parts vendor — not built with the tools above.

cutList.note: Both ends cut 1/2° off square, ends not parallel — mark long point.
Both ends cut 1/2° off square, ends not parallel.
Long point to short point, both ends 1/2° off square, ends parallel.
Both ends 1/2° off square, not parallel.

steps.title: Cut the parts and mark every angle
steps.body:
Cut eight 19" leg wraps from 1x4 and eight more 19" wraps from 1x6, ripped to suit the post you actually received. From the rest of the 1x4 cut the headboard main rails at 58-1/8" and 57-3/4" and two vertical fillers at 24"; from the 1x3 cut the 50-7/8" trim rail and three 10-3/4" fillers. Cut two 63-1/2" side rail top supports and two 81-1/2" side rails from 2x3 — one part per 8 ft board — plus two 79" bottom support rails and the 81-3/4" center support from 2x4, the 79" and 56" 2x12 panel boards (both 79s come off the 16 ft board, both 56s off the 12 ft), and the 30" x 57" back panel from 1/4" plywood. The four 8 ft 1x4s cover their parts with only about half an inch of slack in total, so cut the two long headboard rails first.

Write the long point and the short point on every 1/2°-off-square cut the moment it leaves the saw. At half a degree you cannot see which end is which, and the cut list is specific about which parts have parallel ends (the 24" fillers) and which do not (both headboard main rails and the trim rail).

steps.title: Wrap the legs
steps.body: Each post takes four 19" wraps: two 1x4s at their full 3-1/2" width on opposite faces, and two ripped from 1x6 wide enough to cover the post plus the 3/4" thickness of those first two boards at each side. Measure your actual post before ripping — the legs are a purchased part and 3-1/2" is the nominal base, not a promise, and the cut list's 3-1/2" figure for the ripped boards would leave the wrap open. Bevel or mitre the top edge of the wrap where it meets the turning above, so the transition reads as one piece rather than a collar. Glue and pin-nail one face at a time, keeping each wrap tight to the post as you go.
materials: Pre-turned bed post legs, ~80" length, 3-1/2" base

steps.title: Add the bottom and top rails to the legs
steps.body: Pocket-hole a 79" 2x4 bottom support rail between each pair of wrapped legs, low enough that it will end up under the mattress platform rather than in the sleeper's way. Add a 63-1/2" 2x3 top support to the headboard pair the same way; some builds leave it off the footboard end for a more open look and nothing structural depends on it. Drill 1-1/2" pocket holes and drive 2-1/2" screws into the leg wraps rather than into the turned post — the wrap is what gives you flat, square material to screw to. Check each assembly across the diagonals before the screws go tight.

steps.title: Build the headboard main panel
steps.body: Cut the 58-1/8" long rail and the 57-3/4" short rail with both ends 1/2° off square and NOT parallel, marking long point and short point on every end as you cut. That 3/8" difference between the two rails is the entire angle — it is what makes the headboard splay slightly instead of sitting dead square, and a rail installed end-for-end looks perfectly fine until nothing else fits. Dry-fit both rails between the leg assemblies before you drill a single pocket hole, then assemble with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" screws.

steps.title: Fill in the headboard sides
steps.body: Fit the two 24" vertical fillers between the main rails, both ends 1/2° off square and parallel to each other so each filler sits square to the rails it lands on. Measure the actual gap at each position rather than trusting the 24" figure — the angled rails mean the openings are not identical left to right. Pocket-hole them from the back with 1-1/4" screws so no fastener shows on the face, and glue every joint.

steps.title: Fill in the vertical trim
steps.body: The 1x3 trim rail is 50-7/8" with both ends 1/2° off square and not parallel; the three 10-3/4" fillers divide the run below it into four openings. Fit the rail first, then measure each filler position individually before cutting it to length, for the same reason as the last step. Attach everything with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket hole screws driven from the back face.

steps.title: Attach the back panel
steps.body: Set the 30" x 57" 1/4" plywood panel into the back of the headboard, centered on the framing so an even margin shows all round. Brad-nail it to the rails and fillers about every 6"; it carries no load, but it closes the openings so the headboard does not read as a bare frame from the bedroom door. If you are using the optional cove moulding, run it around the panel's edge to hide the plywood edge grain.

steps.title: Build the footboard
steps.body: The footboard repeats the headboard exactly — wrapped legs, a bottom support rail, angled main rails, fillers — but stops short of the headboard's height. The plan gives no separate footboard cut list, so take the width off the assembled headboard and set the height where the mattress and bedding will still show above it. Keep the same 1/2°-off-square rule and the same long-point marking discipline; both ends of the bed have to lean the same way or the side rails will not sit parallel.

steps.title: Finish separately, then assemble in the room
steps.body: Finish the headboard, footboard, and rails separately before final assembly — it's much easier to move the pieces into the bedroom unassembled than to maneuver one finished frame through a doorway. Assemble with 1-1/2" pocket holes and 2-1/2" pocket hole screws.

steps.title: Add mattress support
steps.body: Screw a cleat along the inside face of each 81-1/2" side rail with 2-1/2" screws, set at whatever height puts the top of your box spring where you want it. Run the 81-3/4" 2x4 center support down the middle from headboard to footboard and put a leg under its midpoint; at queen width that support earns its keep whether you use a box spring or slats. If you are going without a box spring, lay slats across the cleats and the center support instead. Note that neither the cleats nor any slats appear in the cut list or the materials list, so buy that stock separately.

images.alt: A queen canopy or poster bed built around turned pencil-post legs
